title: Training language models to follow instructions with human feedback

paragraphs.text:
## **Training language models to follow instructions with human feedback**

**Long Ouyang** _[∗]_ **Jeff Wu** _[∗]_ **Xu Jiang** _[∗]_ **Diogo Almeida** _[∗]_ **Carroll L. Wainwright** _[∗]_ **Pamela Mishkin** _[∗]_ **Chong Zhang Sandhini Agarwal Katarina Slama Alex Ray John Schulman Jacob Hilton Fraser Kelton Luke Miller Maddie Simens Amanda Askell** _[†]_ **Peter Welinder Paul Christiano** _[∗†]_ **Jan Leike** _[∗]_ **Ryan Lowe** _[∗]_

OpenAI

## **Abstract**

Making language models bigger does not inherently make them better at following a user’s intent. For example, large language models can generate outputs that are untruthful, toxic, or simply not helpful to the user. In other words, these models are not _aligned_ with their users. In this paper, we show an avenue for aligning language models with user intent on a wide range of tasks by fine-tuning with human feedback. Starting with a set of labeler-written prompts and prompts submitted through a language model API, we collect a dataset of labeler demonstrations of the desired model behavior, which we use to fine-tune GPT-3 using supervised learning. We then collect a dataset of rankings of model outputs, which we use to further fine-tune this supervised model using reinforcement learning from human feedback. We call the resulting models _InstructGPT_ . In human evaluations on our prompt distribution, outputs from the 1.3B parameter InstructGPT model are preferred to outputs from the 175B GPT-3, despite having 100x fewer parameters. Moreover, InstructGPT models show improvements in truthfulness and reductions in toxic output generation while having minimal performance regressions on public NLP datasets. Even though InstructGPT still makes simple mistakes, our results show that fine-tuning with human feedback is a promising direction for aligning language models with human intent.

## **1 Introduction**

Large language models (LMs) can be prompted to perform a range of natural language processing (NLP) tasks, given some examples of the task as input. However, these models often express unintended behaviors such as making up facts, generating biased or toxic text, or simply not following user instructions (Bender et al., 2021; Bommasani et al., 2021; Kenton et al., 2021; Weidinger et al., 2021; Tamkin et al., 2021; Gehman et al., 2020). used for many recent large LMs—predicting the next token on a webpage from the internet—is different from the objective “follow the user’s instructions helpfully and safely” (Radford et al., 2019; Brown et al., 2020; Fedus et al., 2021; Rae et al., 2021; Thoppilan et al., 2022). Thus, we say that the language modeling objective is _misaligned_ . Averting these unintended behaviors is especially important for language models that are deployed and used in hundreds of applications.

We make progress on aligning language models by training them to act in accordance with the user’s intention (Leike et al., 2018). This encompasses both explicit intentions such as following instructions and implicit intentions such as staying truthful, and not being biased, toxic, or otherwise harmful. Using the language of Askell et al. (2021), we want language models to be _helpful_ (they should help the user solve their task), _honest_ (they shouldn’t fabricate information or mislead the user), and _harmless_ (they should not cause physical, psychological, or social harm to people or the environment). We elaborate on the evaluation of these criteria in Section 3.5.

We focus on _fine-tuning_ approaches to aligning language models. Specifically, we use reinforcement learning from human feedback (RLHF; Christiano et al., 2017; Stiennon et al., 2020) to fine-tune GPT-3 to follow a broad class of written instructions (see Figure 2). This technique uses human preferences as a reward signal to fine-tune our models. We first hire a team of 40 contractors to label our data, based on their performance on a screening test (see Section 3.3 and Appendix B.1 for more details). We then collect a dataset of human-written demonstrations of the desired output behavior on (mostly English) prompts submitted to a language model API and some labeler-written prompts, and use this to train our supervised learning baselines. Next, we collect a dataset of human-labeled comparisons between outputs from our models on a larger set of API prompts. We then train a reward model (RM) on this dataset to predict which model output our labelers would prefer. Finally, we use this RM as a reward function and fine-tune our supervised learning baseline to maximize this reward using the PPO algorithm (Schulman et al., 2017). We illustrate this process in Figure 2. This procedure aligns the behavior of GPT-3 to the stated preferences of a specific group of people (mostly our labelers and researchers), rather than any broader notion of “human values”; we discuss this further in Appendix G.2. We call the resulting models _InstructGPT_ .

We mainly evaluate our models by having our labelers rate the quality of model outputs on our test set, consisting of prompts from held-out users (who are not represented in the training data). We also conduct automatic evaluations on a range of public NLP datasets. We train three model sizes (1.3B, 6B, and 175B parameters), and all of our models use the GPT-3 architecture. Our main findings are:
**Labelers significantly prefer InstructGPT outputs over outputs from GPT-3.** Outputs from the 1.3B parameter InstructGPT model are preferred to outputs from the 175B GPT-3, despite having

2

over 100x fewer parameters. These models have the same architecture, and differ only by the fact that InstructGPT is fine-tuned on our human data. This result holds true even when we add a few-shot prompt to GPT-3 to make it better at following instructions. Outputs from our 175B InstructGPT are preferred to 175B GPT-3 outputs 85 _±_ 3% of the time, and preferred 71 _±_ 4% of the time to few-shot 175B GPT-3. InstructGPT also generates more appropriate outputs according to our labelers.

**InstructGPT models show improvements in truthfulness over GPT-3.** On the TruthfulQA benchmark, InstructGPT generates truthful and informative answers more often than GPT-3. On “closed-domain” tasks from our API prompt distribution, where the output should not contain information that is not present in the input, InstructGPT models make up information not present in the input about half as often as GPT-3 (a 21% vs. 41% hallucination rate, respectively).

**InstructGPT shows small improvements in toxicity over GPT-3, but not bias.** To measure toxicity, we use the RealToxicityPrompts dataset (Gehman et al., 2020) and conduct both automatic and human evaluations. InstructGPT models generate about 25% fewer toxic outputs than GPT-3 when prompted to be respectful. InstructGPT does not significantly improve over GPT-3 on the Winogender (Rudinger et al., 2018) and CrowSPairs (Nangia et al., 2020) datasets.

**We can minimize performance regressions on public NLP datasets by modifying our RLHF fine-tuning procedure.** During RLHF fine-tuning, we observe performance regressions compared to GPT-3 on certain public NLP datasets. We can greatly reduce the performance regressions on these datasets by mixing PPO updates with updates that increase the log likelihood of the pretraining distribution (PPO-ptx), without compromising labeler preference scores.

**Our models generalize to the preferences of “held-out” labelers that did not produce any training data.** To test the generalization of our models, we conduct a preliminary experiment with held-out labelers, and find that they prefer InstructGPT outputs to outputs from GPT-3 at about the same rate as our training labelers. However, more work is needed to study how these models perform on broader groups of users, and how they perform on inputs where humans disagree about the desired behavior.

**Public NLP datasets are not reflective of how our language models are used.** We compare GPT-3 fine-tuned on our human preference data (i.e. InstructGPT) to GPT-3 fine-tuned on two different compilations of public NLP tasks: the FLAN (Wei et al., 2021) and T0 (Sanh et al., 2021) (in particular, the T0++ variant). These datasets consist of a variety of NLP tasks, combined with natural language instructions for each task. On our API prompt distribution, our FLAN and T0 models perform slightly worse than our SFT baseline, and labelers significantly prefer InstructGPT to these models.

**InstructGPT models show promising generalization to instructions outside of the RLHF finetuning distribution.** We qualitatively probe InstructGPT’s capabilities, and find that it is able to follow instructions for summarizing code, answer questions about code, and sometimes follows instructions in different languages, despite these instructions being very rare in the fine-tuning distribution. This result is exciting because it suggests that our models are able to generalize the notion of “following instructions.” They retain some alignment even on tasks for which they get very little direct supervision.

**InstructGPT still makes simple mistakes.** For example, InstructGPT can still fail to follow instructions, make up facts, give long hedging answers to simple questions, or fail to detect instructions with false premises.

Overall, our results indicate that fine-tuning large language models using human preferences significantly improves their behavior on a wide range of tasks, though much work remains to be done to improve their safety and reliability.

## **2 Related work**

**Research on alignment and learning from human feedback.** We build on previous techniques to align models with human intentions, particularly reinforcement learning from human feed-

3

Blue arrows indicate that this data is used to train one of our models. In Step 2, boxes A-D are samples from our models that get ranked by labelers.

back (RLHF). Originally developed for training simple robots in simulated environments and Atari games (Christiano et al., 2017; Ibarz et al., 2018), it has recently been applied to fine-tuning language models to summarize text (Ziegler et al., 2019; Stiennon et al., 2020; Böhm et al., 2019; Wu et al., 2021). This work is in turn influenced by similar work using human feedback as a reward in domains such as dialogue (Jaques et al., 2019; Yi et al., 2019; Hancock et al., 2019), translation (Kreutzer et al., 2018; Bahdanau et al., 2016), semantic parsing (Lawrence and Riezler, 2018), story generation (Zhou and Xu, 2020), review generation (Cho et al., 2018), and evidence extraction (Perez et al., 2019). In concurrent work, Askell et al. (2021); Bai et al. (2022) propose language assistants as a testbed for alignment research, and train models using RLHF. Our work can be seen as a direct application of RLHF to aligning language models on a broad distribution of language tasks.

**Training language models to follow instructions.** Our work is also related to research on crosstask generalization in language models, where LMs are fine-tuned on a broad range of public NLP datasets (usually prefixed with an appropriate instruction) and evaluated on a different set of NLP tasks. There has been a range of work in this domain (Yi et al., 2019; Mishra et al., 2021; Wei et al., 2021; Khashabi et al., 2020; Sanh et al., 2021; Aribandi et al., 2021), which differ in training and evaluation data, formatting of instructions, size of pretrained models, and other experimental details.

**Mitigating the harms of language models.** A goal of modifying the behavior of language models is to mitigate the harms of these models when they’re deployed in the real world. These risks have been extensively documented (Bender et al., 2021; Bommasani et al., 2021; Kenton et al., 2021; Weidinger et al., 2021; Tamkin et al., 2021). Language models can produce biased outputs (Dhamala et al., 2021; Liang et al., 2021; Manela et al., 2021; Caliskan et al., 2017; Kirk et al., 2021), leak private data (Carlini et al., 2021), generate misinformation (Solaiman et al., 2019; Buchanan et al., 2021), and be used maliciously; for a thorough review we direct the reader to Weidinger et al. (2021). There are many ways to mitigate these harms, including by fine-tuning on a small, valuetargeted dataset (Solaiman and Dennison, 2021), filtering the pretraining dataset (Ngo et al., 2021), or human-in-the-loop data collection (Dinan et al., 2019; Xu et al., 2020).

4

## **3 Methods and experimental details**

## **3.1 High-level methodology**

Our methodology follows that of Ziegler et al. (2019) and Stiennon et al. (2020), who applied it in the stylistic continuation and summarization domains. We start with a pretrained language model (Radford et al., 2019; Brown et al., 2020; Fedus et al., 2021; Rae et al., 2021; Thoppilan et al., 2022), a distribution of prompts on which we want our model to produce aligned outputs, and a team of trained human labelers (see Section 3.3 for details). We then apply the following three steps (Figure 2).

**Step 1: Collect demonstration data, and train a supervised policy.** Our labelers provide demonstrations of the desired behavior on the input prompt distribution (see Section 3.2 for details on this distribution). We then fine-tune a pretrained GPT-3 model on this data using supervised learning.

**Step 2: Collect comparison data, and train a reward model.** We collect a dataset of comparisons between model outputs, where labelers indicate which output they prefer for a given input. We then train a reward model to predict the human-preferred output.

**Step 3: Optimize a policy against the reward model using PPO.** We use the output of the RM as a scalar reward. We fine-tune the supervised policy to optimize this reward using the PPO algorithm (Schulman et al., 2017).

Steps 2 and 3 can be iterated continuously; more comparison data is collected on the current best policy, which is used to train a new RM and then a new policy. In practice, most of our comparison data comes from our supervised policies, with some coming from our PPO policies.

## **3.2 Dataset**

Our prompt dataset consists primarily of text prompts submitted to a commercial language model API, as well as a small number of labeler-written prompts. These prompts are very diverse and include generation, question answering, dialog, summarization, extractions, and other natural language tasks (see Appendix A). Our dataset is over 96% English. We heuristically deduplicate prompts, and ensure that the validation and test sets contain no data from users whose data is in the training set. We also filter prompts containing personally identifiable information (PII).

From these prompts, we produce three different datasets used in our fine-tuning procedure: (1) our SFT dataset, with labeler demonstrations used to train our SFT models, (2) our RM dataset, with labeler rankings of model outputs used to train our RMs, and (3) our PPO dataset, without any human labels, which are used as inputs for RLHF fine-tuning. The SFT dataset contains about 13k training prompts (from the API and labeler-written), the RM dataset has 33k training prompts (from the API and labeler-written), and the PPO dataset has 31k training prompts (only from the API). More details on dataset sizes are provided in Table 3.

## **3.3 Human data collection**

To produce our demonstration and comparison data, and to conduct our main evaluations, we hired a team of about 40 contractors on Upwork and through ScaleAI. Compared to earlier work that collects human preference data on the task of summarization (Ziegler et al., 2019; Stiennon et al., 2020; Wu et al., 2021), our inputs span a much broader range of tasks, and can occasionally include controversial and sensitive topics. Our aim was to select a group of labelers who were sensitive to the preferences of different demographic groups, and who were good at identifying outputs that were potentially harmful. Thus, we conducted a screening test designed to measure labeler performance on these axes (see Appendix B.1). As an initial study to see how well our model generalizes to the preferences of other labelers, we hire a separate set of labelers who do not produce any of the training data. These labelers are sourced from the same vendors, but do not undergo a screening test.

Despite the complexity of the task, we find that inter-annotator agreement rates are quite high: training labelers agree with each-other 72 _._ 6 _±_ 1 _._ 5% of the time, while for held-out labelers this number is 77 _._ 3 _±_ 1 _._ 3%. For comparison, in the summarization work of Stiennon et al. (2020) researcher-researcher agreement was 73 _±_ 4%.

5

## **3.4 Models**

Starting from GPT-3 (Brown et al., 2020), we train models with three different techniques:

**Supervised fine-tuning (SFT).** We fine-tune GPT-3 on our labeler demonstrations using supervised learning. We trained for 16 epochs, using a cosine learning rate decay, and residual dropout of 0.2. We do our final SFT model selection based on the RM score on the validation set. Similarly to Wu et al. (2021), we find that our SFT models overfit on validation loss after 1 epoch; however, we find that training for more epochs helps both the RM score and human preference ratings.

**Reward modeling (RM).** We fine-tune GPT-3 to take in a prompt and response, and output a scalar reward. In this paper we only use 6B RMs, as this saves a lot of compute, and we found that 175B RM training could be unstable and thus was less suitable to be used as the value function during RL (see Appendix D for more details).

In Stiennon et al. (2020), the RM is trained on a dataset of comparisons between two model outputs on the same input. They use a cross-entropy loss, with the comparisons as labels—the difference in rewards represents the log odds that one response will be preferred to the other by a human labeler. In order to speed up comparison collection, we have labelers rank between _K_ = 4 and _K_ = 9 responses, and train on all � _K_ 2 � comparisons from each prompt as a single batch element, for computational efficiency (see Appendix D. where _rθ_ ( _x, y_ ) is the scalar output of the reward model for prompt _x_ and completion _y_ with parameters _θ_ , _yw_ is the preferred completion out of the pair of _yw_ and _yl_ , and _D_ is the comparison dataset.

**Reinforcement learning (RL).** Again following Stiennon et al. (2020), we fine-tuned the SFT model using PPO (Schulman et al., 2017). The environment is a bandit environment which presents a random user prompt and expects a response to the prompt. Given the prompt and response, it produces a reward determined by the reward model and ends the episode. In addition, we add a per-token KL penalty from the SFT model at each token to mitigate over-optimization of the reward model. The value function is initialized from the RM. We call these models “PPO.”

We also experiment with mixing the pretraining gradients into the PPO gradients, in order to fix the performance regressions on public NLP datasets (see Appendix D.4). We call these models “PPO-ptx.” Unless otherwise specified, in this paper InstructGPT refers to the PPO-ptx models. **Baselines.** We compare the performance of our PPO models to our SFT models and GPT-3. We also compare to GPT-3 when it is provided a few-shot prefix to ‘prompt’ it into an instruction-following mode (GPT-3-prompted). This prefix is prepended to the user-specified instruction.

We additionally compare InstructGPT to fine-tuning 175B GPT-3 on the FLAN (Wei et al., 2021) and T0 (Sanh et al., 2021) datasets, which both consist of a variety of NLP tasks, combined with natural language instructions for each task (they differ in the NLP datasets included, and the style of instructions used). We fine-tune them on approximately 1 million examples and choose the checkpoint which obtains the highest RM score on the validation set (see Appendix D for more details).

## **3.5 Evaluation**

Following Askell et al. (2021), we say our models are aligned if they are helpful, truthful, and harmless (we elaborate in Appendix C.2). We divide our quantitative evaluations into two parts:

**Evaluations on API distribution.** Our main metric is human preference ratings on a held out set of prompts from the same source as our training distribution. When using prompts from the API for evaluation, we only select prompts by users we haven’t included in training. For each model we calculate how often its outputs are preferred to a baseline policy; we choose our 175B SFT model as the baseline since its performance is near the middle of the pack. Additionally, we ask labelers to judge the overall quality of each response on a 1-7 Likert scale and collect a range of metadata for each model output (see Table 11). In particular, we collect data that aims to capture different

**Evaluations on public NLP datasets.** We evaluate on two types of public datasets: those that capture an aspect of language model safety, particularly truthfulness, toxicity, and bias, and those that capture zero-shot performance on traditional NLP tasks like question answering, reading comprehension, and summarization. We also conduct human evaluations on the RealToxicityPrompts dataset (Gehman et al., 2020).

## **4 Results**

## **4.1 Results on the API distribution**

**Labelers significantly prefer InstructGPT outputs over outputs from GPT-3.** On our test set, our labelers significantly prefer InstructGPT outputs across model sizes (Figure 1). We find that GPT-3 outputs perform the worst, and one can obtain significant step-size improvements by using a well-crafted few-shot prompt (GPT-3 (prompted)), then by training on demonstrations using supervised learning (SFT), and finally by training on comparison data using PPO. Adding updates on the pretraining mix during PPO does not lead to large changes in labeler preference. To illustrate the magnitude of our gains: when compared directly, 175B InstructGPT outputs are preferred to GPT-3 outputs 85 _±_ 3% of the time, and preferred 71 _±_ 4% of the time to few-shot GPT-3.

In Figure 4 we show that labelers also rate InstructGPT outputs favorably along several more concrete axes. Specifically, compared to GPT-3, InstructGPT outputs are more appropriate in the context of a customer assistant, more often follow explicit constraints defined in the instruction (e.g. “Write your answer in 2 paragraphs or less.”), are less likely to fail to follow the correct instruction entirely, and make up facts (‘hallucinate’) less often in closed-domain tasks.

**Our models generalize to the preferences of "held-out" labelers that did not produce any training data.** Held-out labelers have similar ranking preferences as workers who we used to produce training data (see Figure 3). In particular, according to held-out workers, all of our InstructGPT models still greatly outperform the GPT-3 baselines. Thus, our InstructGPT models aren’t simply overfitting to the preferences of our training labelers.

**Public NLP datasets are not reflective of how our language models are used.** In Figure 5a, we also compare InstructGPT to our 175B GPT-3 baselines fine-tuned on the FLAN (Wei et al., 2021) and T0 (Sanh et al., 2021) datasets (see Appendix D for details). We find that these models perform better than GPT-3, on par with GPT-3 with a well-chosen prompt, and worse than our SFT baseline. This indicates that these datasets are not sufficiently diverse to improve performance on our API prompt distribution. We believe this is partly because academic datasets focus on tasks where performance is easily measured, like classification and QA, while our API distribution consists of mostly (about 57%) open-ended generation tasks.

## **4.2 Results on public NLP datasets**

**InstructGPT models show improvements in truthfulness over GPT-3.** As measured by human evaluations on the TruthfulQA dataset, our PPO models show small but significant improvements in generating truthful and informative outputs compared to GPT-3 (see Figure 5b). This behavior is the default: our models do not have to be specifically instructed to tell the truth to exhibit improved truthfulness. Interestingly, the exception is our 1.3B PPO-ptx model, which performs slightly worse than a GPT-3 model of the same size. Our improvements in truthfulness are also evidenced by the fact that our PPO models hallucinate less often on closed-domain tasks (Figure 4).

**InstructGPT shows small improvements in toxicity over GPT-3, but not bias.** We first evaluate our models on the RealToxicityPrompts dataset (Gehman et al., 2020) using human evaluations. Our results are in Figure 5c. We find that, when instructed to produce a safe and respectful output (“respectful prompt”), InstructGPT models generate less toxic outputs than those from GPT-3 according to the Perspective API. This advantage disappears when the respectful prompt is removed (“no prompt”). We see similar results when evaluating using the Perspective API (Appendix F.7).

**We can minimize performance regressions on public NLP datasets by modifying our RLHF fine-tuning procedure.** In Figure 25 we show that adding pretraining updates to our PPO finetuning (PPO-ptx) mitigates performance regressions on public NLP datasets, and even surpasses GPT-3 on HellaSwag. The performance of the PPO-ptx model still lags behind GPT-3 on DROP, SQuADv2, and translation; more work is needed to study and further eliminate these performance regressions. We also find that mixing in pretraining updates performs better than the simpler solution of increasing the KL coefficient (Figure 36).

8

## **4.3 Qualitative results**

**InstructGPT models show promising generalization to instructions outside of the RLHF finetuning distribution.** In particular, we find that InstructGPT shows ability to follow instructions in non-English languages, and perform summarization and question-answering for code. This is interesting because non-English languages and code form a tiny minority of our fine-tuning data, and it suggests that, in some cases, alignment methods could generalize to producing the desired behavior on inputs that humans did not directly supervise. We show some qualitative examples in Figure 26.

**InstructGPT still makes simple mistakes.** In interacting with our 175B PPO-ptx model, we have noticed it can still make simple mistakes, despite its strong performance on many different language tasks. To give a few examples: (1) when given an instruction with a false premise, the model sometimes incorrectly assumes the premise is true, (2) the model can overly hedge; when given a simple question, it can sometimes say that there is no one answer to the question and give multiple possible answers, even when there is one fairly clear answer from the context, and (3) the model’s performance degrades when instructions contain multiple explicit constraints (e.g. “list 10 movies made in the 1930’s set in France”) or when constraints can be challenging for language models (e.g. writing a summary in a specified number of sentences).

We show some examples of these behaviors in Figure 27. We suspect that behavior (2) emerges partly because we instruct labelers to reward epistemic humility; thus, they may tend to reward outputs that hedge, and this gets picked up by our reward model. We suspect that behavior (1) occurs because there are few prompts in the training set that assume false premises, and our models don’t generalize well to these examples. We believe both these behaviors could be dramatically reduced with adversarial data collection (Dinan et al., 2019).

## **5 Discussion**

## **5.1 Implications for alignment research**

Our approach to alignment research in this work is iterative: we are improving the alignment of current AI systems instead of focusing abstractly on aligning AI systems that don’t yet exist, which provides us with a clear empirical feedback loop of what works and what does not. We believe that this feedback loop is essential to refine our alignment techniques, and it forces us to keep pace with progress in machine learning.

From this work, we can draw lessons for alignment research more generally. First, the cost of increasing model alignment is modest relative to pretraining. Training our 175B SFT model requires 4.9 petaflops/s-days and training our 175B PPO-ptx model requires 60 petaflops/s-days, compared to 3,640 petaflops/s-days for GPT-3 (Brown et al., 2020). At the same time, our results show that RLHF is very effective at making language models more helpful to users, more so than a 100x model size increase. This suggests that right now increasing investments in alignment of existing language models is more cost-effective than training larger models. Second, we’ve seen some evidence that InstructGPT generalizes ‘following instructions’ to settings that we don’t supervise it in. This is an important property because it’s prohibitively expensive to have humans supervise models on every task they perform. Finally, we were able to mitigate most of the performance degradations introduced by our fine-tuning. If this was not the case, these performance degradations would constitute an alignment tax—an additional cost for aligning the model. Any alignment technique with a high tax might not see adoption, and thus such a tax is important to avoid.

## **5.2 Limitations**

**Methodology.** The behavior of our InstructGPT models is determined in part by the human feedback obtained from our contractors. Some of the labeling tasks rely on value judgments that may be impacted by the identity of our contractors, their beliefs, cultural backgrounds, and personal history. We kept our team of contractors small because this facilitates high-bandwidth communication with a smaller set of contractors who are doing the task full-time. However, this group is clearly not representative of the full spectrum of people affected by these models. As a simple example, our labelers are primarily English-speaking and our data consists almost entirely of English instructions.

9

**Models.** Our models are neither fully aligned nor fully safe; they still generate toxic or biased outputs, make up facts, and generate sexual and violent content without explicit prompting. They can also fail to generate reasonable outputs on some inputs; we show some examples of this in Figure 27. Perhaps the greatest limitation of our models is that, in most cases, they follow the user’s instruction, even if that could lead to harm in the real world. For example, when prompting the models to be maximally biased, InstructGPT generates more toxic outputs than equivalently-sized GPT-3 models.

## **5.3 Broader impacts**

This work is motivated by our aim to increase the positive impact of large language models by training them to do what a given set of humans want them to do. By default, language models optimize the next word prediction objective, which is only a proxy for what we want these models to do. Our results indicate that our techniques hold promise for making language models more helpful, truthful, and harmless. In the longer term, alignment failures could lead to more severe consequences, particularly if these models are deployed in safety-critical situations.

However, making language models better at following user intentions also makes them easier to misuse. It may be easier to use these models to generate convincing misinformation, or hateful or abusive content. Alignment techniques are not a panacea for resolving safety issues associated with large language models; rather, they should be used as one tool in a broader safety ecosystem. Aside from intentional misuse, there are many domains where large language models should be deployed only with great care, or not at all. Examples include high-stakes domains such as medical diagnoses, classifying people based on protected characteristics, determining eligibility for credit, employment, or housing, generating political advertisements, and law enforcement.

Finally, the question of who these models are aligned to is extremely important, and will significantly affect whether the net impact of these models is positive or negative; we discuss this in Appendix G.2.

coarse__gemini-3-flash-preview.label: Coarse (gemini-3-flash-preview)
coarse__gemini-3-flash-preview.overall_feedback:
The paper introduces InstructGPT, a method for aligning large language models with human intent using reinforcement learning from human feedback (RLHF). By fine-tuning GPT-3 on a combination of demonstration data and preference rankings, the authors demonstrate that a 1.3B parameter model can outperform a 175B parameter baseline in following instructions while reducing toxicity and hallucinations.

This work is a significant milestone in AI alignment, providing a scalable framework for moving beyond next-token prediction toward helpful and safe assistants. The methodology is thorough, and the empirical results regarding parameter efficiency and cross-task generalization are highly compelling.

Overview issues:

- Ambiguity in Inter-Rater Reliability and Preference Aggregation: In Section 3.3, the authors report inter-annotator agreement rates of approximately 73-77%. While these numbers are provided, the paper lacks a detailed analysis of the variance in these rankings across different demographic or cultural subgroups within the labeler pool. If a significant portion of the 40 contractors shared similar cultural backgrounds, the 'human preference' signal might be a narrow reflection of a specific subculture rather than a generalizable human value. The authors should include a breakdown of agreement by task type (e.g., creative writing vs. factual QA) and discuss how the reward model handles cases where labelers have fundamental disagreements on subjective prompts.

- Potential for Reward Model Over-optimization (Goodhart's Law): Section 3.4 describes the PPO training phase but does not sufficiently address the risks of the policy 'gaming' the reward model. As noted in the literature context regarding scaling laws for reward models, policies often exploit flaws in the proxy reward to achieve high scores that do not actually correlate with human preference. While the KL penalty mentioned in Section 3.4 (Step 3) is a standard mitigation, the paper lacks a sensitivity analysis on the KL coefficient. The authors should provide evidence showing at what point the reward model's signal becomes unreliable and how they ensured the 175B model did not converge to 'sycophantic' or overly verbose behaviors that trick the reward model.

- Opaque Selection and Diversity of API Prompt Distribution: The methodology relies heavily on prompts submitted to the OpenAI API (Section 3.2). However, the criteria for 'heuristically deduplicating' and filtering these prompts are not fully transparent. There is a risk that the distribution is heavily skewed toward specific use cases (e.g., brainstorming or customer service) that do not represent the full range of human-AI interaction. To bolster the claim of general instruction following, the authors should provide a more granular taxonomy of the prompt categories and demonstrate that the model's performance gains are consistent across all major categories, rather than being driven by a few high-frequency task types.

- Stability and Hyperparameter Sensitivity of PPO: The paper notes in Section 3.4 that 175B Reward Model training was unstable and thus a 6B model was used instead. This suggests significant sensitivity in the RLHF pipeline that is not fully explored. The authors should detail the specific failure modes encountered with the larger reward models. Furthermore, the stability of the PPO phase for the 175B policy is a major technical hurdle; the paper would be much stronger if it included a discussion of the hyperparameter tuning required to prevent policy collapse or divergence during the RL phase, which is a common issue in large-scale RLHF.

- Superficial Metrics for Truthfulness and Safety: The improvements in truthfulness and toxicity (Section 4.2) rely on TruthfulQA and RealToxicityPrompts. As the authors admit in Section 4.3, the model still makes simple mistakes and can be 'tricked' by false premises. There is a concern that RLHF might be optimizing for the *appearance* of truthfulness—such as a confident or hedging tone—rather than factual accuracy. The authors should include an analysis of whether the model's 'hallucination rate' reduction is due to better fact retrieval or simply a learned bias toward more cautious, generic responses that are harder to label as 'false' by humans.

- Missing recovery test for the SFT baseline in the RLHF pipeline: The paper claims that RLHF is superior to SFT, but it doesn't verify if the RLHF process can recover the performance of a high-quality SFT model when the reward signal is perfectly aligned with the SFT data. A common failure mode in PPO is the 'forgetting' of supervised patterns. The authors should include a sanity check where the Reward Model is trained strictly on the SFT demonstrations to see if the PPO policy converges to the SFT baseline's performance or if the RL noise causes a regression. Specifically, computing the KL-divergence between the SFT model and a PPO model trained on a 'perfect' RM would clarify the efficiency of the optimization.

- Absence of a 'No-Instruction' control for TruthfulQA gains: The paper reports improved truthfulness on TruthfulQA but fails to isolate whether this is due to the alignment process or simply the model learning to recognize the 'format' of the benchmark. To show this result has bite, the authors should compare the InstructGPT results against a version of GPT-3 that is fine-tuned on a generic, non-instructional dataset of similar size (e.g., a subset of C4). This would demonstrate that the truthfulness gains are a product of the human feedback loop rather than a byproduct of any additional fine-tuning on high-quality text. Without this, it is unclear if the 'alignment' is doing the work or if any secondary training phase would yield similar results.

- Underdeveloped implementation details for the PPO-ptx gradient mixing: The 'PPO-ptx' model is presented as a solution to the alignment tax, yet the paper lacks the specific mixing ratio or weighting logic used to combine RL gradients with pretraining gradients. Since this is a core contribution to maintaining performance on public NLP datasets, the absence of the objective function's hyperparameter (the weight beta for the pretraining log-likelihood term) makes the result impossible to replicate. The authors should provide the exact formulation of the PPO-ptx loss and a plot showing how different mixing ratios affect the trade-off between human preference scores and SQuAD/DROP benchmark performance.

- Missing comparison of RM ranking vs. absolute Likert scoring: The methodology uses a ranking-based Reward Model (Equation 1) but evaluates the final model using 1-7 Likert scales (Figure 5a). There is a structural gap in showing that the RM—trained on relative preferences—actually correlates with the absolute quality metrics used in the results. The paper should include a calibration plot where RM predicted scores are binned against the human-assigned Likert scores for the same completions. This would verify that the RM is a valid proxy for the 'quality' the authors claim to improve, rather than just a model of 'comparative preference' which can sometimes favor the 'least bad' option without identifying 'good' ones.

Recommendation: accept

Revision targets:

- Include a detailed breakdown of inter-rater agreement across different task categories and demographic groups.

- Provide a sensitivity analysis of the KL penalty coefficient to demonstrate the trade-off between reward optimization and policy stability.

- Add a more granular taxonomy and distribution analysis of the API-sourced prompt dataset.

- Document the specific technical challenges and hyperparameter settings that led to the decision to use a 6B reward model over a 175B version.

comments.title: Ambiguity in Value Function Initialization
comments.quote: The value function is initialized from the RM. We call these models “PPO.”
comments.explanation: The claim that the value function is initialized from the Reward Model (RM) is underspecified because the RM and the value function (VF) have different output dimensions and head architectures. The RM is trained on a ranking loss to output a scalar representing the preference of a full completion, whereas the VF in PPO must output a scalar estimate of the expected future return at each individual token step. If the RM was trained as a sequence-level classifier, its final layer weights are not directly compatible with a per-token value head without a specific mapping. The transformer backbone can be shared, but the scalar head of a ranking RM and a PPO value head require different initialization logic. Clarify whether the scalar head was re-initialized or if a specific pooling strategy was used to map sequence-level rewards to token-level values.

comments.title: Contradiction in PPO Environment Definition
comments.quote: The environment is a bandit environment which presents a random user prompt and expects a response to the prompt. Given the prompt and response, it produces a reward determined by the reward model and ends the episode.
comments.explanation: The text describes the RL setup as a 'bandit environment' where the episode ends after a single response, but then states that a 'per-token KL penalty' is applied. In a standard bandit formulation, the action is the entire sequence and the reward is a single scalar. However, applying a per-token penalty and using a value function implies a Markov Decision Process (MDP) where each token is an individual action. If the environment were truly a bandit at the sequence level, a value function would be redundant as there are no intermediate states to evaluate. The use of PPO and a token-level KL penalty confirms the underlying structure is a sequential MDP. The description should be updated to reflect this sequential nature to avoid confusing readers familiar with RL theory.

comments.title: Missing Formal Definition of PPO-ptx Objective
comments.quote: We can greatly reduce the performance regressions on these datasets by mixing PPO updates with updates that increase the log likelihood of the pretraining distribution (PPO-ptx)
comments.explanation: The claim that PPO-ptx reduces performance regressions relies on a specific mixing of updates, but the paper lacks the mathematical formulation of the combined objective. In standard RLHF, the objective is the expected reward minus a KL penalty; adding a pretraining term introduces a new hyperparameter to weight the log-likelihood of the pretraining data. Without the formal equation showing how the RL gradient and the supervised pretraining gradient are combined, the term 'mixing' is mathematically underspecified. This makes the results impossible to replicate. Provide the exact formulation of the PPO-ptx loss and the specific coefficient used for the pretraining log-likelihood term.

comments.title: Selection Bias in Baseline Checkpoint Selection
comments.quote: We fine-tune them on approximately 1 million examples and choose the checkpoint which obtains the highest RM score on the validation set
comments.explanation: Selecting the best checkpoint for FLAN and T0 baselines based on the Reward Model (RM) score introduces a significant evaluation bias. Since the RM is trained specifically to predict human preferences on the InstructGPT prompt distribution, using it as the selection criterion for external baselines forces those models to align with the InstructGPT RM's specific biases rather than their own native optimization targets. This likely underestimates the performance of FLAN/T0 on their own terms. Clarify if other metrics like validation loss were considered for checkpoint selection to ensure the baseline is not being handicapped by a proxy metric it was not designed for.

comments.title: Inconsistency in Inter-Annotator Agreement Rates
comments.quote: training labelers agree with each-other $72.6\pm 1.5\%$ of the time, while for held-out labelers this number is $77.3\pm 1.3\%$.
comments.explanation: The reported agreement rates are counter-intuitive. One would expect the 'training labelers'—who passed a specific screening test for performance and sensitivity—to exhibit higher agreement than the 'held-out labelers' who did not undergo any screening. Instead, the unscreened held-out group shows a significantly higher agreement rate (77.3%) compared to the screened training group (72.6%). This discrepancy implies that either the screening test actually decreased consensus or that the held-out group is more homogeneous in its biases. Explain this inverse relationship or verify if the values for the two groups were accidentally swapped.

comments.title: Conflation of Truthfulness and Contextual Faithfulness
comments.quote: On “closed-domain” tasks from our API prompt distribution, where the output should not contain information that is not present in the input, InstructGPT models make up information not present in the input about half as often as GPT-3 (a 21% vs. 41% hallucination rate, respectively).
comments.explanation: The section links TruthfulQA (factual accuracy) with closed-domain hallucination rates (adherence to provided context) as unified evidence of 'truthfulness.' This is a category error: a model can be highly 'truthful' on TruthfulQA while being 'unfaithful' in a closed-domain task by ignoring the provided context in favor of its internal weights. The reduction in hallucination rate could simply be an improvement in following the instruction to stay within the context, rather than an improvement in veracity. Distinguish between 'factual accuracy' and 'contextual faithfulness' to avoid conflating instruction-following with underlying truthfulness.

comments.title: Ambiguity in User-Based Data Splitting
comments.quote: We heuristically deduplicate prompts, and ensure that the validation and test sets contain no data from users whose data is in the training set.
comments.explanation: Splitting by user ID rather than by prompt content creates a risk of data leakage if different users submitted identical prompts (e.g., common queries like 'Write a story about a cat'). It is unclear if deduplication was performed globally across the entire corpus before the user-based split or only within each split. If deduplication was not global, the validation set could contain prompts semantically identical to the training set, artificially inflating performance. Confirm whether deduplication was performed across the entire dataset prior to the user-based partition.

comments.title: Incomplete Sentence in Literature Review
comments.quote: We build on previous techniques to align models with human intentions, particularly reinforcement learning from human feed
comments.explanation: The opening sentence of Section 2 is cut off mid-word ('feed') and does not conclude before the page break. This disrupts the logical flow of the literature review and leaves the primary methodological foundation of the paper grammatically incomplete. Ensure the sentence is fully typed and concludes with a period.

coarse__glm-4.6.label: Coarse (glm-4.6)
coarse__glm-4.6.overall_feedback:
This paper introduces InstructGPT, a method for aligning language models with user intent through reinforcement learning from human feedback (RLHF). The authors demonstrate that a 1.3B parameter InstructGPT model outperforms the 175B GPT-3 on human preference evaluations, while also showing improvements in truthfulness and toxicity. The methodology involves three stages: supervised fine-tuning on demonstrations, reward model training on human rankings, and RL fine-tuning using PPO. While the results are impressive, there are significant concerns about the evaluation methodology, generalization claims, and the framing of alignment.

The paper makes a valuable contribution by demonstrating that RLHF can significantly improve instruction following and alignment behaviors even in much smaller models. The three-stage training pipeline is well-executed and the empirical results on human preferences are compelling. However, the paper overstates its claims about generalization and underestimates the limitations of its evaluation methodology, particularly the reliance on a narrow group of labelers and the disconnect between API prompts and real-world deployment scenarios.

Overview issues:

- Limited representativeness of human labelers: The paper acknowledges that the 40 contractors who provided all training feedback are not representative of the broader population, but this limitation is not adequately addressed in the evaluation or discussion. The labelers are primarily English-speaking and were selected based on a screening test designed by the researchers. This creates a significant alignment problem: the models are aligned to the preferences of this specific, homogeneous group rather than to broader human values. The held-out labeler experiment (Section 4.1) only tests generalization to similar contractors from the same vendors, not to truly diverse populations. This undermines the paper's claims about aligning models with 'user intent' more broadly.

- Reward model hacking is not empirically investigated: The paper mentions adding a KL penalty to mitigate over-optimization of the reward model, but provides no systematic analysis of whether the models are actually gaming the reward signal. Given the known issue of reward hacking in RLHF systems (as noted in the literature context), the absence of concrete evidence or analysis on this front is concerning. The authors should have included experiments probing for reward model exploitation, such as testing whether the model generates outputs that score high on the reward model but would be rated poorly by humans in held-out evaluations. This is particularly important given the complexity of the instruction-following task compared to summarization.

- Evaluation metrics lack rigor for toxicity and bias claims: The paper's claims about toxicity reduction are based primarily on automatic metrics (Perspective API) and limited human evaluation on RealToxicityPrompts. The finding that toxicity improvements disappear without a 'respectful prompt' suggests the model has not fundamentally learned to avoid toxicity but merely responds to explicit instructions. More problematic is the claim that InstructGPT does not improve bias based only on Winogender and CrowSPairs results. These datasets capture only narrow aspects of bias and are insufficient to conclude that the method doesn't address bias. The paper should have included more comprehensive bias evaluations and acknowledged that the single dataset result is not conclusive.

- Insufficient analysis of failure modes: While Section 4.3 mentions that InstructGPT still makes mistakes, the analysis of failure modes is cursory and qualitative. The paper identifies three types of errors (false premises, excessive hedging, constraint violations) but provides no systematic quantification of their frequency or severity. Given the safety implications of alignment failures, a more thorough analysis would have included: (1) quantitative measurement of these failure modes across the evaluation set, (2) analysis of whether certain types of prompts are more prone to failures, and (3) investigation of whether the RL stage introduces new failure modes compared to the SFT baseline. Without this analysis, it's difficult to assess the real-world safety of the deployed model.

- Overstated generalization claims from limited evidence: The paper makes strong claims about generalization to non-English languages and code based on only qualitative examples (Section 4.3). Given that the training data is over 96% English and contains minimal code, these claims require more substantial evidence. The authors should have provided quantitative evaluations on held-out non-English prompts and code tasks to support these generalization claims. As presented, the evidence is anecdotal and insufficient for the strong statements made about the model's ability to generalize 'following instructions' to unseen domains.

- Missing demonstration of reward hacking: The paper mentions adding a KL penalty to mitigate reward model over-optimization but provides no empirical investigation of whether reward hacking actually occurs. Given that reward hacking is a known failure mode in RLHF systems, readers need to see concrete evidence of whether and how the model exploits the reward signal. The authors should include systematic tests, such as analyzing outputs that receive high reward scores but are rated poorly by held-out human evaluators, or measuring reward score inflation during training. Without this analysis, it's unclear whether the KL penalty is actually necessary or sufficient to prevent gaming.

- No quantitative failure mode analysis: While Section 4.3 qualitatively describes three types of errors (false premises, excessive hedging, constraint violations), the paper lacks any systematic quantification of these failure modes. For an alignment paper making safety claims, readers need to know the frequency and severity of these errors across the evaluation set. The authors should provide statistics on how often each failure type occurs, whether certain prompt categories are more prone to failures, and whether the RL stage introduces new failures compared to the SFT baseline. This quantitative analysis is essential for assessing the real-world safety implications.

- Insufficient bias evaluation scope: The paper claims InstructGPT does not improve bias based solely on Winogender and CrowSPairs results. These datasets capture only narrow aspects of gender and stereotypical bias. To substantiate a claim about bias more broadly, the paper needs evaluation across multiple bias dimensions (racial, religious, age, etc.) and using diverse evaluation methods. The authors should include additional bias benchmarks and qualitative analysis of biased outputs. Without more comprehensive evaluation, the claim about lack of bias improvement is not well-supported.

- Missing quantitative generalization evidence: The paper claims generalization to non-English languages and code based only on qualitative examples in Section 4.3. Given that the training data is over 96% English with minimal code, these strong generalization claims require quantitative validation. The authors should evaluate performance on held-out non-English prompts and code tasks, measuring instruction-following accuracy in these domains. Without systematic evaluation, the generalization claims remain anecdotal and insufficient for the paper's strong statements about cross-domain instruction following.

Recommendation: Major revision. The core methodology and results are sound, but the paper overstates its claims and underestimates methodological limitations. The representativeness of the human feedback, the lack of analysis on reward hacking, and the insufficient evaluation of bias and toxicity are significant concerns that must be addressed. The generalization claims need stronger empirical support.

Revision targets:

- Conduct and report comprehensive bias evaluations beyond Winogender and CrowSPairs, including intersectional bias analysis

- Add empirical investigation of reward model hacking behaviors with systematic probing experiments

- Include quantitative evaluation of failure modes with frequency analysis and comparison across training stages

- Provide quantitative evidence for generalization claims to non-English languages and code, not just qualitative examples

- Expand the discussion of labeler representativeness limitations and their implications for deployment

comments.title: Incorrect parameter ratio in abstract
comments.quote: In human evaluations on our prompt distribution, outputs from the 1.3B parameter InstructGPT model are preferred to outputs from the 175B GPT-3, despite having 100x fewer parameters.
comments.explanation: The abstract claims the 1.3B InstructGPT model has 100x fewer parameters than 175B GPT-3, but 175B divided by 1.3B equals approximately 134.6, not 100. This mathematical error should be corrected to 'over 130x fewer parameters' to maintain accuracy.

comments.title: Ambiguous toxicity reduction claim
comments.quote: InstructGPT models generate about 25% fewer toxic outputs than GPT-3 when prompted to be respectful.
comments.explanation: The 25% toxicity reduction lacks critical context. Without knowing the baseline toxicity rate, readers cannot assess whether this reduction is meaningful. For example, a 25% relative reduction from 4% to 3% toxicity is quite different from reducing 40% to 30%. The paper should report both the baseline rate and absolute reduction to properly contextualize this finding.

comments.title: Overgeneralized bias claim from limited datasets
comments.quote: InstructGPT does not significantly improve over GPT-3 on the Winogender *(Rudinger et al., 2018)* and CrowSPairs *(Nangia et al., 2020)* datasets.
comments.explanation: This statement overgeneralizes from two specific bias datasets to conclude about bias broadly. Winogender measures gender bias in occupational associations, while CrowSPairs focuses on stereotypical associations. Neither covers racial, religious, age, or other bias dimensions. The claim should be qualified to specify it applies only to the types of bias measured by these particular datasets.

comments.title: Missing quantitative evidence for held-out labeler generalization
comments.quote: To test the generalization of our models, we conduct a preliminary experiment with held-out labelers, and find that they prefer InstructGPT outputs to outputs from GPT-3 at about the same rate as our training labelers.
comments.explanation: The claim that held-out labelers prefer InstructGPT outputs 'at about the same rate' lacks quantitative evidence. Without reporting the actual preference rates and confidence intervals for both groups, readers cannot assess whether the similarity is meaningful. The paper should provide the specific preference percentages for held-out labelers and compare them directly to the training labeler results.

comments.title: Insufficient evidence for code and language generalization
comments.quote: We qualitatively probe InstructGPT’s capabilities, and find that it is able to follow instructions for summarizing code, answer questions about code, and sometimes follows instructions in different languages, despite these instructions being very rare in the fine-tuning distribution.
comments.explanation: The generalization claim about code and non-English languages is based solely on qualitative probing rather than systematic evaluation. Without quantitative results measuring instruction-following accuracy on held-out code and non-English prompts, the claim that models 'generalize the notion of following instructions' remains anecdotal. The authors should provide systematic evaluation to support this strong claim.

comments.title: Inconsistent baseline selection for API evaluation
comments.quote: For each model we calculate how often its outputs are preferred to a baseline policy; we choose our 175B SFT model as the baseline since its performance is near the middle of the pack.
comments.explanation: This creates an inconsistency with the abstract's claims about 1.3B InstructGPT being preferred to 175B GPT-3. Using SFT (already instruction-tuned) as baseline rather than raw GPT-3 makes the comparison less impressive and potentially masks the full magnitude of improvement. The authors should either use GPT-3 as baseline to match abstract claims, or clarify that different baselines are used for different evaluation contexts.

comments.title: Circular dependency in SFT model selection
comments.quote: We do our final SFT model selection based on the RM score on the validation set. Similarly to *Wu et al. (2021)*, we find that our SFT models overfit on validation loss after 1 epoch; however, we find that training for more epochs helps both the RM score and human preference ratings.
comments.explanation: Selecting SFT models using RM score creates a circular dependency since the RM is trained later using SFT model outputs. The authors should clarify whether they use a pre-trained RM for SFT selection or how they resolve this dependency in their training pipeline. As written, the description suggests a methodological inconsistency.

comments.title: Incomplete loss function specification
comments.quote: $\mathrm{loss}\left(\theta\right)=-\frac{1}{\binom{K}{2}}E_{\left(x,y_{w},y_{l}\right)\sim D}\left[\log\left(\sigma\left(r_{\theta}\left(x,y_{w}\right)-r_{\theta}\left(x,y_{l}\right)\right)\right)\right]$ (1)
comments.explanation: The loss function appears inconsistent with the described batching strategy. The text states that for K responses, they train on all \binom{K}{2} comparisons 'as a single batch element', but the loss function only divides by \binom{K}{2} in the expectation. The equation should either explicitly show the inner sum over all pairwise comparisons for each prompt, or clarify that the expectation is over individual comparison pairs rather than K-response groups.

comments.title: Ambiguous reward model initialization
comments.quote: The value function is initialized from the RM.
comments.explanation: This statement is ambiguous. In PPO, the value function estimates expected returns, while the reward model outputs a scalar reward for a given prompt-response pair. The authors should clarify whether they mean: (1) using the RM's architecture as initialization for the value network, (2) using RM outputs as value targets during initialization, or (3) something else. Without this clarification, the technical description is incomplete.

comments.title: Inconsistent baseline evaluation for FLAN/T0
comments.quote: We fine-tune them on approximately 1 million examples and choose the checkpoint which obtains the highest RM score on the validation set (see Appendix D for more details).
comments.explanation: Using RM score for FLAN/T0 baseline selection creates an unfair comparison since InstructGPT models were optimized for human preferences, not RM scores. This could artificially inflate InstructGPT's apparent superiority. The baseline selection should use the same evaluation metric (human preferences) as the main comparisons to ensure fairness.

comments.title: Dataset size discrepancy needs clarification
comments.quote: The SFT dataset contains about 13k training prompts (from the API and labeler-written), the RM dataset has 33k training prompts (from the API and labeler-written), and the PPO dataset has 31k training prompts (only from the API).
comments.explanation: The relationship between these datasets is unclear. If the RM dataset requires rankings of model outputs, it should be derived from the SFT dataset prompts, not larger. The authors should clarify whether these datasets overlap or are completely separate, and if they overlap, specify the total unique prompt count.

comments.title: Incorrect citation in related work
comments.quote: This work is in turn influenced by similar work using human feedback as a reward in domains such as dialogue (Jaques et al., 2019; Yi et al., 2019; Hancock et al., 2019), translation (Kreutzer et al., 2018; Bahdanau et al., 2016), semantic parsing (Lawrence and Riezler, 2018), story generation (Zhou and Xu, 2020), review generation (Cho et al., 2018), and evidence extraction (Perez et al., 2019).
comments.explanation: The citation (Bahdanau et al., 2016) is incorrectly categorized. This paper introduced attention mechanisms for neural machine translation, not human feedback as a reward signal. Remove (Bahdanau et al., 2016) from this list as it doesn't fit the category of 'using human feedback as a reward.'

comments.title: Inconsistent model naming convention
comments.quote:
els “PPO.”

We also experiment with mixing the pretraining gradients into the PPO gradients, in order to fix the performance regressions on public NLP datasets (see Appendix D.4). We call these models “PPO-ptx.” Unless otherwise specified, in this paper InstructGPT refers to the PPO-ptx models.

#### Baselines.
comments.explanation: The naming convention could confuse readers. The text states that InstructGPT refers to PPO-ptx models, but earlier sections discuss results from both PPO and PPO-ptx variants. The authors should clarify which results correspond to which variant to avoid misinterpretation.

comments.title: Mathematical error in hallucination rate comparison
comments.quote:
main” tasks from our API prompt distribution, where the output should not contain information that is not present in the input, InstructGPT models make up information not present in the input about half as often as GPT-3 (a 21% vs. 41% hallucination rate, respectively).

#### Instruc
comments.explanation: The claim that InstructGPT hallucinates 'about half as often' as GPT-3 is mathematically incorrect. 21% is approximately 51% of 41%, not 50%. The claim should be corrected to 'about half' if 21% was rounded from ~20.5%, or stated as '51% as often' for accuracy.

coarse__qwen3-235b-a22b-2507.label: Coarse (qwen3-235b-a22b-2507)
coarse__qwen3-235b-a22b-2507.overall_feedback:
The paper presents a compelling and influential application of reinforcement learning from human feedback (RLHF) to align language models with user intent. Its core contribution—demonstrating that a much smaller model (1.3B) can outperform a vastly larger one (175B) in human preference evaluations—is impactful and has shaped subsequent work in alignment. The methodology is generally sound, building on established techniques, and the empirical results are strong across multiple axes: preference alignment, truthfulness, and toxicity reduction. The transparency around data collection and model training adds credibility.

Overview issues:

- Insufficient discussion of reward model over-optimization risks: The paper applies PPO to optimize against a reward model trained on human rankings, but does not adequately address the risk of over-optimization—where the policy learns to exploit artifacts in the reward model rather than genuinely improve alignment. This is a known failure mode in RLHF, yet the authors do not present diagnostics such as reward model accuracy on held-out comparisons or ablations showing whether performance gains plateau or reverse with longer training. Without these, it's unclear whether the observed improvements reflect true alignment or reward hacking. The authors should include analysis of reward model calibration on the final policy’s outputs and discuss how KL penalties mitigate but may not eliminate this risk.

- Limited generalization evidence beyond contractor preferences: While the paper claims that InstructGPT generalizes to held-out labelers, the evaluation uses labelers from the same vendors and without a screening test, which does not establish robustness to diverse user populations. The broader concern—that alignment reflects the values of a narrow group of contractors—is acknowledged in Appendix G.2 but not rigorously tested. Readers will question whether the model generalizes to users with different cultural, linguistic, or ethical backgrounds. A more meaningful test would involve labelers from demographically diverse regions or with differing political views to probe sensitivity to value-laden prompts.

- Overstated truthfulness improvements due to metric limitations: The paper claims InstructGPT improves truthfulness, citing human evaluations on TruthfulQA. However, the authors themselves note in the acknowledgments that automatic metrics overstated these gains—a significant caveat buried late in the paper. This undermines confidence in the magnitude of improvement, especially since the 1.3B PPO-ptx model performs worse than its GPT-3 counterpart. The claim would be stronger with a breakdown of which question types show gains versus regressions, and whether improvements stem from hedging (e.g., saying 'I don’t know') rather than correct factual recall. This distinction is critical for assessing real-world reliability.

- Narrow prompt distribution limits external validity: The training and evaluation data are drawn almost entirely from API submissions and labeler-written prompts in English, which constrains the scope of alignment. The paper acknowledges this but does not quantify how representative this distribution is of real-world use cases. For example, code-related and non-English instructions are rare in training, yet the model is expected to generalize to them. A more rigorous analysis would include failure modes on low-resource languages or domains absent from training, rather than qualitative examples alone. This limitation affects claims about broad instruction-following capabilities.

- Missing ablation of RLHF components: The paper does not isolate the contribution of each stage—supervised fine-tuning (SFT), reward modeling (RM), and PPO—to final performance. For instance, how much of the improvement comes from SFT alone versus the RL loop? Without this ablation, it's unclear whether the complexity of RLHF is necessary or if simpler methods (e.g., DPO) could achieve similar results. The inclusion of FLAN and T0 baselines helps, but a direct comparison between SFT-only, RM-only, and full PPO would strengthen the argument for the full pipeline.

- No ablation of RLHF component contributions: The paper claims that fine-tuning with human feedback improves alignment, but does not quantify how much each stage—supervised fine-tuning (SFT), reward modeling (RM), and PPO—contributes to the final performance. Readers will want to know whether the gains come primarily from SFT, or if the RLHF loop provides substantial additional value. This matters because if SFT alone accounts for most of the improvement, the complexity of training a reward model and running PPO may not be justified. The paper should include a direct comparison of SFT-only models, RM-only scoring (without PPO), and full PPO models on the same evaluation metrics, ideally in a single figure showing stepwise gains. Without this, the necessity of the full RLHF pipeline remains unclear.

- Missing analysis of reward model calibration post-optimization: The paper uses a reward model to guide PPO fine-tuning but provides no evidence that the reward model remains well-calibrated on the final InstructGPT outputs. In RLHF, policies can learn to exploit reward model inaccuracies—a phenomenon known as reward hacking—leading to outputs that score well but are not actually preferred. A natural check is to evaluate the reward model’s accuracy on a held-out set of human comparisons involving PPO-generated outputs. The authors should report this accuracy and, ideally, show how it changes across PPO training steps. If accuracy drops, it suggests over-optimization; if stable, it strengthens confidence in the results. This is standard practice in RLHF work and its absence leaves a key assumption untested.

- No breakdown of truthfulness gains by question type: The paper reports aggregate improvements on TruthfulQA but does not analyze whether gains are concentrated in specific categories—e.g., science, health, or conspiracy theories—or whether the model improves by answering correctly or by hedging (e.g., saying 'I don’t know'). This distinction is important because increased hedging could inflate truthfulness scores without improving factual accuracy. Readers will wonder if the model is actually more truthful or just more cautious. The authors should provide a category-wise breakdown of performance and include metrics on 'I don’t know' rates for InstructGPT versus GPT-3. This would clarify the mechanism behind the improvement and assess real-world utility.

- Lack of failure mode analysis on out-of-distribution instructions: While the paper notes that InstructGPT generalizes to code and non-English instructions, it offers only qualitative examples rather than systematic evaluation. Given that these domains are rare in training data, readers will question how reliably the model handles them. A minimal requirement is to define a small test set of non-English prompts and code-related tasks, then report human or automated evaluation scores on these subsets. This would show whether the observed generalization is robust or anecdotal. Without such analysis, claims about broad instruction-following capabilities rest on weak evidence.

Recommendation: major revision

Revision targets:

- Provide evidence of reward model generalization, including calibration curves and accuracy on policy-generated samples to rule out over-optimization.

- Expand the generalization analysis to include demographically diverse evaluators or at least discuss the limitations of current labeler diversity more critically.

- Revise the truthfulness claims to reflect the acknowledged overstatement of gains, with a detailed breakdown of performance by question type on TruthfulQA.

- Include a full ablation study comparing SFT, RM, and PPO contributions to final performance, ideally with a DPO or direct preference baseline.

- Quantify the representativeness of the API prompt distribution and analyze failure modes on out-of-distribution tasks such as non-English instructions or rare domains.

comments.title: Unsubstantiated claim of no compromise in labeler preferences
comments.quote: During RLHF fine-tuning, we observe performance regressions compared to GPT-3 on certain public NLP datasets. We can greatly reduce the performance regressions on these datasets by mixing PPO updates with updates that increase the log likelihood of the pretraining distribution (PPO-ptx), without compromising labeler preference scores.
comments.explanation: The claim that PPO-ptx reduces performance regressions 'without compromising labeler preference scores' lacks empirical support in the text. While the method mitigates regressions on public datasets, no data is presented to confirm that human evaluators still prefer PPO-ptx outputs over alternatives. It is possible that preserving pretraining distribution likelihood reintroduces undesirable behaviors (e.g., verbosity or inaccuracy) that reduce alignment quality. To justify this assertion, the authors should report direct human comparisons between PPO and PPO-ptx models on the API prompt distribution. Without such evidence, the claim risks implying a free lunch in alignment trade-offs.

comments.title: Incorrect normalization in reward model loss function
comments.quote:
The loss function for the RM becomes:

$\mathrm{loss}\left(\theta\right)=-\frac{1}{\binom{K}{2}}E_{\left(x,y_{w},y_{l}\right)\sim D}\left[\log\left(\sigma\left(r_{\theta}\left(x,y_{w}\right)-r_{\theta}\left(x,y_{l}\right)\right)\right)\right]$ (1)
comments.explanation: The normalization factor $\frac{1}{\binom{K}{2}}$ is applied outside the expectation over dataset $D$, but since $D$ already includes all $\binom{K}{2}$ pairwise comparisons generated from each $K$-way ranking, this leads to double-counting. Each pair is sampled independently during training, so adding an explicit average per prompt down-weights updates when multiple comparisons come from the same input. This distorts gradient magnitudes depending on $K$, potentially destabilizing training. The standard approach treats each pair as an independent example. The loss should drop the normalization constant: $\mathrm{loss}(\theta) = -E_{(x,y_w,y_l)\sim D}\left[\log\sigma\left(r_\theta(x,y_w) - r_\theta(x,y_l)\right)\right]$, which aligns with typical pairwise preference learning implementations.

comments.title: KL penalty phrasing obscures directionality
comments.quote: we add a per-token KL penalty from the SFT model at each token to mitigate over-optimization of the reward model.
comments.explanation: The phrase 'KL penalty from the SFT model' is ambiguous and may mislead readers about the direction of regularization. In practice, the KL term is $\mathrm{KL}(\pi_\theta || \pi_\mathrm{SFT})$, penalizing deviations of the current policy from the supervised fine-tuned model. The wording 'from the SFT model' could incorrectly suggest the reverse (SFT diverging from current) or imply the SFT model actively penalizes. This matters because the correct interpretation ensures the policy stays close to human demonstrations. Rewriting as 'a per-token KL penalty relative to the SFT model' or explicitly stating $\mathrm{KL}(\pi_\theta || \pi_\mathrm{SFT})$ would eliminate confusion and clarify the regularization mechanism.

comments.title: Bandit environment characterization misrepresents sequential structure
comments.quote: The environment is a bandit environment which presents a random user prompt and expects a response to the prompt. Given the prompt and response, it produces a reward determined by the reward model and ends the episode.
comments.explanation: Describing the setup as a 'bandit environment' is misleading. A multi-armed bandit assumes no internal state or sequence structure—each action is atomic. But here, the model generates a response token-by-token, making it a sequential decision process with delayed reward, i.e., a Markov Decision Process (MDP) with a single reward at termination. While common in RLHF, calling it a bandit oversimplifies and may mislead readers about the applicability of bandit-specific algorithms or theoretical guarantees. A more accurate description would be 'a single-episode environment with delayed scalar reward' or 'an episodic RL setting with terminal feedback', preserving correctness while reflecting standard framing in the literature.

comments.title: Ambiguous reference to 'SFT model' in figure caption
comments.quote: Figure 1: Human evaluations of various models on the API prompt distribution, evaluated by how often outputs from each model were preferred to those from the 175B SFT model. Our InstructGPT models (PPO-ptx) as well as its variant trained without pretraining mix (PPO) significantly outperform the GPT-3 baselines (GPT, GPT prompted); outputs from our 1.3B PPO-ptx model are preferred to those from the 175B GPT-3.
comments.explanation: The caption refers to the '175B SFT model' as the baseline for preference comparisons, but does not define what this model is. From context, it appears to be the supervised fine-tuned model trained on human demonstrations but before RLHF. However, without clarification, readers might confuse it with GPT-3 or assume it is part of the InstructGPT family. Since the caption contrasts it with GPT-3 and PPO variants, explicitly stating that it is 'the 175B supervised fine-tuned model, trained only on human demonstrations without reinforcement learning' would prevent misinterpretation and clarify the evaluation hierarchy.

comments.title: Incomplete description of PPO optimization loop
comments.quote:
We use the output of the RM as a scalar reward. We fine-tune the supervised policy to optimize this reward using the PPO algorithm *(Schulman et al., 2017)*.

Steps 2 and 3 can be iterated continuously; more comparison data is collected on the current best policy, which is used to train a new RM and then a new policy. In practice, most of our comparison data comes from our supervised policies, with some coming from our PPO policies.
